author: @andrewlongfield3102
text: Very nicely put, it's refreshing to see someone honest commenting on an impossible project. As soon as I saw this the first thing that sprang to mind was that this contravenes Newtons first law of motion, 'a body will remain in motion until acted upon by another force'. In this case this is not a closed circuit, and so there are outside forces acting upon it, namely friction, and so the inertia of this system will decay as it is turned into heat energy by the friction acting on it. This also relates to the conservation of energy / mass law, which states that you can neither create or destroy energy, only change it's state, but the mass/ energy in the system will remain constant.  The reason these ideas are described as 'laws' rather than theorems is because they have been subjected to centuries of rigorous testing by the finest minds of the times to be reasonably certain that they (the laws), do not fail under any observable conditions or tests. The idea that a geography teacher from America has somehow found a set of circumstances where these laws cease to operate is unlikely to say the least. As much as I, like many others, would like access to free endless power, if and when it's ever discovered, it won't be something that breaks fundamental laws of physics, and it will take an exceptional mind (like Nicholas Tesla, for example), to discover it.

author: @kristiaanverberne8419
text:
I investigated the Quick Power Generator and brought the price down from 50 to 10 USD which was acceptable for me to look into it. To start with, the whole principle in that book laughs with Newton's law that you never can get more energy out than you provide. More specific, because you always get a loss, whatever you do, you will always get less out. So the whole theory is completely fraudulent and doesn't work.

However, I have my own theory about how to build an off the grid generator. But it is just academic, a kind of brainstorming. Let us say you have a generator which delivers power by 1500 rpm and connect that with a motor which does 3000 rpm which is a ratio of 2:1. I put a 2:1 gearbox between them. So in theory, the one drives the other forever. Regretfully, because of the friction, which causes a loss, this isn't true.

But what happens when we can compensate for the loss of energy by the friction of the gearbox but also the losses in the motor and generator? Let's add solar panels together with a rechargeable solar battery, to the motor/generator which compensates for the losses. As long as there is extra power (panels/battery) to compensate for the losses, this system can work.

However, let us assume that none of the parts wears out over time (which is impossible), will this gives us everlasting energy? To be honest, besides calculations, the only method to find out is... building it small scale, experimenting and seeing yourself.

It's an interesting subject. Any idea yourself?

replies.author: @hubertcumberdale4385
replies.text:
A flywheel would essentially perform the same function as the capacitor bank, as an energy store. It would basically cause you to need more energy to start the system and then keep it spinning for slightly longer when you remove the energy supply in, but energy will still be lost through friction and electrical resistance.

That's almost besides the the point though. Let's say theoretically you have a machine that is 100% efficient - the motor turns the generator which powers the motor and it goes on forever. Even with this impossible machine, you cannot use it to power anything indefinitely because the generator can never produce more power than the motor feeds it. So if you start up the system with 10W flowing through it and plug in a 5W lightbulb, only 5W is left for the motor, so only 5W is generated, which is then used by the bulb and before you know it everything stops. Therefore the system is effectively just a very inefficient and low capacity energy store - you will only ever get less energy out than you use to start it up.

replies.author: @lewisgoudy863
replies.text:
@hubertcumberdale4385 "A flywheel would essentially perform the same function as the capacitor bank, as an energy store."
No, a spring does that. A flywheel has as its electrical analog a coil. Both store energy, but a capacitor uses the stored energy to try to maintain the voltage, while a coil uses it to try to maintain the current. Look up "reactance", "capacitive reactance", and "inductive reactance" to learn the difference. The third analogy is that of "resistance" and "dashpot".

replies.author: @hubertcumberdale4385
replies.text: @lewisgoudy863 Yes that's true that a flywheel is more analogous to an inducter than a capacitor. Kind of besides the point in this context though since they both store energy, they just do it in different ways.

replies.author: @michaelmoorrees3585
replies.text:
Alternators ARE Generators. Generator is the more generic term, while alternator is used in the automotive industry. The term "alternator" was used because and alternators magnetic parts generate AC, which are then rectified with semiconductor rectifiers, that are now (within the last 50 years) part of the alternator assembly. In the old days (1960s and earlier) the car's generators output DC, by mechanical rectification, using brushes, that flip phases to ensure that the polarity was always the same. This also require a "cut-out" that disconnected the generator from the car battery, when the engine was in idle. Otherwise, the battery would then power the generator, turning into a motor. By the 60's, semiconductor technology was durable enough to use them for rectification. The result was the ALTERNATOR, which was smaller and needed no cut-out. But both the older style "generator" and alternator co-existed in the automotive industry for a couple of decades, hence the need for two different terms.

When I was in engineering school, we often referred to them as "machines", as most generators, and motors, can be used as either. In practice though, they are optimized for one or the other.

The  temperature at which it becomes solid is even colder, about about 14 degrees Kelvin above absolute zero).  The only other form hydrogen can take is an isotope, which tends to irradiate those nearby with neutron particles.  Our sun does create a new form of hydrogen by smashing their nuclei together at tremendous pressures. author: @stevetobias4890
text:
I hate scammers that try to dupe people with little knowledge to spend a bit of money for nothing. Any motor that drives a generator will put out less energy than the motor requires to run. This is the fundamental principle due to outside forces such as friction. The only way around this is using wind our moving water that doesn't cost you anything. This is why I loved your energy harvesting projects.
Simply, if it seems too good to be true, it quite often is.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh I agree with that mate

author: @xspotbox4400
text:
I love philosophy of energy, it's a game where you can never win, you can never get even, can't even quit the game.
Let's suppose we use a small Sterling engine, powered by a candle, to turn one of those perpetual motion Archimedes wheels, can we use enhanced rotation of a much larger wheel to produce another candle? In principle we could, since candle is a natural material, created by waste energy systems like sun, air and waters. So our partially gravity powered wheel could provide work necessary for making a candle from natural sources. It's how we get all energy in the world anyway.
I have no idea how people imagined Easy generator, but power consumption control is a clever idea. We need to think about efficient distribution and learn got to use grid only to kick of various transformations, eliminating as many looses as possible and regenerate energy wherever we can. It's more a general mentality problem than some technical barriers industry couldn't overcome, nobody want to think about and take care for another empty belly all the time.
